core_claim: Hermes Agent — Nous Research's open-source (MIT), self-hosted autonomous agent — is exploding in popularity because of a self-improving learning loop that turns your tasks into reusable skills it curates over time; it's genuinely powerful, but an always-on agent with a terminal and your keys is a new surface to scope, not fear.

caption:
An open-source AI agent quietly became one of the most-used agents in the world, and outside dev circles almost nobody noticed.

Hermes Agent, from Nous Research, launched February 25 and has blown past 180,000 GitHub stars since — MIT-licensed, running on your own box. The hook is the learning loop: finish a hard task and it writes a reusable skill from the conversation, then loads that skill next time instead of relearning. It keeps curating those skills as you go, so it gets sharper the longer you use it. That's the genuinely cool part.

Here's the part I keep flagging. This is an always-on agent with a terminal, web access, and your API keys. That's a real new surface, not a reason to panic. Run it in one of its sandbox backends, give it least-privilege keys, log what it touches, and treat community-shared skills like untrusted code — because skills execute.

And 'self-improving' isn't the sci-fi version. It curates skill files; it doesn't rewrite its own brain. Useful and real, minus the hype.

Would you hand an autonomous agent your terminal if it never left your machine?

Save this before your team installs it. Follow for AI security without the fake panic.
comment_prompt: Would you trust an autonomous agent with your terminal if it stayed fully on your machine — yes with a sandbox, or no?
alt_text: Slide 1 (cover): a server box in the dark with a glowing orb orbiting it; headline reads that the agent which writes its own skills is eating GitHub.
Slide 2 (context): a glowing cube on a desk with roots of light spreading out; text says an open-source agent that lives on your server and learns as it works.
Slide 3 (the surge): a steep arc of light climbing almost vertically; text says launched Feb 25, past 180,000 GitHub stars and about 1,000 contributors.
Slide 4 (the special sauce): a glowing loop pressing tools into skill tablets onto a growing stack; text says it reads your work, writes a reusable skill, and reuses it next time.
Slide 5 (security reality): an open glowing doorway with keys and cables spilling out; text says it's an always-on agent with a terminal, web access, and your API keys.
Slide 6 (scope it): a glowing cube sealed in a reinforced container with one controlled gate; text says run it in a sandbox, give it least-privilege keys, log what it touches.
Slide 7 (fine print): a magnifying glass over an engraved glowing tablet; text says self-improving means it curates skill files, not rewriting its own brain.
Slide 8 (the shift): a glowing cube cabled into a home workstation; text says a capable persistent agent now runs on your own box and you can swap any model.
Slide 9 (takeaway): a calm hand resting on a glowing contained cube; text says the win is an agent you own and can contain.
Slide 10 (CTA): a glowing arrow and question mark over a dark desk; text asks whether you'd give an autonomous agent your terminal if it never left your machine.

sources.supports: MIT license; self-hosted self-improving agent with a learning loop that writes/curates reusable skills; messaging platforms; sandbox execution backends; model-agnostic; 180,000+ GitHub stars; v0.15.x.

'The agent that grows with you'; persistent memory and auto-generated skills; sandbox backends with container hardening and namespace isolation; positioned against IDE copilots and chatbot wrappers; runs on your own server.

sources.source: NVIDIA Blog — Hermes self-improving agents on RTX PCs and DGX Spark
sources.supports: Learning loop: 'Hermes writes and refines its own skills... saves its learnings as a skill so it can adapt and improve over time'; built to run locally on-device.

sources.source: The Decoder — Nous Research releases Hermes Desktop
sources.supports: Open-source MIT agent across messaging platforms; persistent memory, sub-agents with terminals, sandboxed execution backends; Hermes Desktop public preview (June 2026).

sources.source: TechTimes — Hermes Agent dethrones OpenClaw on OpenRouter
sources.supports: Reportedly became the most-used agent on OpenRouter's daily inference ranking in May 2026 (usage/ranking claim — verify live).

narration.text: An open-source AI agent just became one of the most-used agents in the world.
Hermes Agent. Launched in February, already past a hundred and eighty thousand GitHub stars.
Its trick: it turns your tasks into reusable skills and gets sharper the longer it runs.
But it's an always-on agent with a terminal and your keys, so sandbox it and scope the keys.
Would you give an agent your terminal if it never left your machine?

beats.caption: An open-source agent just became one of the most-used agents in the world.
Launched in February. Already past 180,000 GitHub stars.
Its trick: it turns your tasks into reusable skills and compounds over time.
Always-on, with a terminal and your keys — so sandbox it and scope the keys.
Would you give an agent your terminal if it never left your machine?
